items.label: Keywords to Pattern Map
items.code:
"subarray, contiguous, window"    -> Sliding Window / Prefix Sum
"substring + condition"            -> Sliding Window + HashMap
"sorted array + search"            -> Binary Search
"top k, kth largest"               -> Heap / Bucket Sort
"all combinations, permutations"   -> Backtracking
"shortest path, minimum steps"     -> BFS (unweighted) / Dijkstra
"connected, regions, islands"      -> DFS / BFS / Union-Find
"parentheses, brackets, nesting"   -> Stack
"next greater, next smaller"       -> Monotonic Stack
"duplicate, seen before"           -> Set / HashMap
"min cost, max profit, # of ways" -> Dynamic Programming
"merge intervals, overlapping"     -> Sort + Scan
"prefix, word dictionary"          -> Trie
"locally optimal, scheduling"      -> Greedy
items.note: Read the problem statement and match keywords to narrow down the pattern before coding.

items.label: Constraints to Complexity
items.code:
n <= 20        -> O(2^n) or O(n!)   -> Backtracking, Bitmask DP
n <= 500       -> O(n^2) or O(n^3)  -> DP (2D), nested loops
n <= 10^5      -> O(n log n)        -> Sort, Heap, Binary Search
n <= 10^6      -> O(n)              -> HashMap, Sliding Window, Prefix Sum
n <= 10^9+     -> O(log n) or O(1)  -> Binary Search, Math
items.note: Always check constraints first. They tell you the expected time complexity, which eliminates many patterns.

items.label: 5-Step Problem Solving Checklist
items.code:
# 1. Understand: restate the problem, clarify edge cases
# 2. Match: use keywords + constraints to pick a pattern
# 3. Plan: write pseudocode using the pattern template
# 4. Code: translate pseudocode to Python
# 5. Verify: trace through examples, check edge cases
#    - empty input, single element, duplicates, negatives
items.note: Never skip step 2. Pattern matching is the highest-leverage skill in interviews.

items.label: Trigger
items.code:
Keywords: sorted array, pair sum, in-place, remove duplicates, palindrome
Structure: linear data, two ends converging or slow/fast
items.note: Works on sorted arrays or when you need to compare elements from both ends.

items.label: Data Structures
items.code:
# Sorted array or string
# Two index variables: left, right (or slow, fast)
# No extra space needed — O(1) space pattern
items.note: No extra space needed. This is an O(1) space pattern.

items.label: Template
items.note: O(n) time, O(1) space. Array must be sorted for this variant.

items.label: Example 1 — LC 167 Two Sum II (Sorted)
items.note: Classic converging pointers. Guaranteed exactly one solution.

items.label: Example 2 — LC 11 Container With Most Water
items.note: Move the shorter side inward — moving the taller side can never increase area.

items.label: Key Insight
items.code:
# Why move the pointer with the smaller value?
# The bottleneck is the shorter line. Moving the taller
# line inward only shrinks width without helping height.
# So we always move the shorter pointer to search for
# a taller line that might increase the area.
items.note: Two pointers reduces O(n^2) brute force to O(n) by eliminating impossible pairs.

items.label: Trigger
items.code:
Keywords: subarray, substring, contiguous, window, at most k, longest, shortest
Structure: contiguous sequence with a constraint
items.note: If the problem says 'contiguous subarray/substring' + optimization, think sliding window.

items.label: Data Structures
items.code:
from collections import defaultdict
# HashMap (Counter) for frequency tracking
window = defaultdict(int)
# Two pointers: left, right defining window [left, right]
items.note: The window expands right and contracts left to maintain a valid state.

items.label: Template
items.code:
def sliding_window(s):
    window = defaultdict(int)
    left = 0
    ans = 0
    for right in range(len(s)):
        window[s[right]] += 1          # expand
        while window_invalid():         # shrink
            window[s[left]] -= 1
            left += 1
        ans = max(ans, right - left + 1)  # update
    return ans
items.note: O(n) time — each element enters and leaves the window at most once.

items.label: Example 1 — LC 3 Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
items.note: Track last-seen index. Jump left pointer past the duplicate.

items.label: Example 2 — LC 567 Permutation in String
items.note: Fixed-size window of len(s1). Compare frequency maps.

items.label: Key Insight
items.code:
# Fixed window: size is known -> slide by adding right, removing left
# Variable window: size unknown -> expand right, shrink left until valid
# Always ask: what makes the window invalid?
items.note: The shrink condition is the core of every sliding window problem.

items.label: Trigger
items.code:
Keywords: sorted, search, minimum/maximum that satisfies, rotated sorted
Structure: monotonic property — if f(mid) is true, all values beyond also true
items.note: Binary search applies anytime there is a monotonic decision boundary.

items.label: Data Structures
items.code:
# Sorted array or implicit search space [lo, hi]
# Three pointers: lo, hi, mid
# Can also binary search on the answer (e.g., minimum speed)
items.note: Binary search on the answer transforms optimization into a decision problem.

items.label: Template
items.note: lo <= hi for exact match. lo < hi for boundary search. Know both variants.

items.label: Example 1 — LC 704 Binary Search
items.note: Textbook binary search. O(log n) time, O(1) space.

items.label: Example 2 — LC 875 Koko Eating Bananas
items.note: Binary search on the answer. Search space is [1, max(piles)]. Monotonic: higher speed -> fewer hours.

items.label: Key Insight
items.code:
# Binary search on answer: define a feasibility function
# f(x) that is monotonic (False...False, True...True).
# Then find the first x where f(x) is True.
# This transforms optimization into a decision problem.
items.note: Whenever you see 'minimum value that satisfies X', think binary search on the answer.

items.label: Trigger
items.code:
Keywords: parentheses, brackets, valid nesting, next greater, next smaller, stock span
Structure: nested or hierarchical processing, monotonic relationships
items.note: If you need to match pairs or find next-greater/smaller elements, use a stack.

items.label: Data Structures
items.code:
# Python list as stack: append() to push, pop() to pop
stack = []  # stores indices or values
# Monotonic stack: maintain increasing or decreasing order
items.note: Monotonic stack processes elements and pops when the monotonic property is violated.

items.label: Template (Monotonic Stack — Next Greater)
items.note: O(n) — each element is pushed and popped at most once.

items.label: Example 1 — LC 20 Valid Parentheses
items.note: Push openers, pop on matching closer. Stack must be empty at end.

items.label: Example 2 — LC 739 Daily Temperatures
items.note: Monotonic decreasing stack. When a warmer day appears, pop and record the gap.

items.label: Key Insight
items.code:
# Matching problems -> basic stack (push/pop pairs)
# Next greater/smaller -> monotonic stack
# Monotonic decreasing: pop when current > top (finds next greater)
# Monotonic increasing: pop when current < top (finds next smaller)
items.note: Monotonic stacks solve 'next greater/smaller' in O(n) instead of O(n^2).

items.label: Trigger
items.code:
Keywords: shortest path (unweighted), level order, minimum steps, nearest
Structure: graph or grid, exploring layer by layer
items.note: BFS guarantees shortest path in unweighted graphs. Use Dijkstra for weighted.

items.label: Data Structures
items.code:
from collections import deque
queue = deque()    # FIFO queue
visited = set()    # track visited nodes
items.note: Always use deque for O(1) popleft. Never use list — pop(0) is O(n).

items.label: Template
items.note: The for-loop over len(queue) processes one level at a time.

items.label: Example 1 — LC 200 Number of Islands (BFS)
items.note: Flood-fill with BFS. Mark visited by setting to '0' (in-place).

items.label: Example 2 — LC 994 Rotting Oranges
items.note: Multi-source BFS — start from all rotten oranges simultaneously.

items.label: Key Insight
items.code:
# Single-source BFS: one starting point
# Multi-source BFS: add ALL sources to queue before starting
# Grid BFS: use directions = [(0,1),(0,-1),(1,0),(-1,0)]
# Level tracking: process len(queue) nodes per iteration
items.note: Multi-source BFS is just BFS with multiple starting points in the initial queue.

items.label: Trigger
items.code:
Keywords: explore all paths, tree traversal, connected components, cycle detection
Structure: graph or tree, depth-first exploration
items.note: DFS when you need to explore all paths or process subtrees. BFS when you need shortest path.

items.label: Data Structures
items.code:
# Recursion stack (implicit) or explicit stack
# visited set for graphs (trees don't need it)
visited = set()
# Watch recursion depth: sys.setrecursionlimit(10**6)
items.note: Use sys.setrecursionlimit or iterative DFS for large inputs.

items.label: Template
items.note: Graph DFS needs visited. Tree DFS does not (no cycles in trees).

items.label: Example 1 — LC 104 Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
items.note: Base case: null -> 0. Recursive case: 1 + max of children. Simplest tree DFS.

items.label: Example 2 — LC 200 Number of Islands (DFS)
items.note: Flood-fill with DFS. Same result as BFS but recursive. Mark cells '0' to avoid revisiting.

items.label: Key Insight
items.code:
# DFS on trees: usually postorder (process children first)
# DFS on graphs: need visited set to avoid infinite loops
# Grid DFS: bounds check + visited check at top of recursion
# Iterative DFS: use explicit stack (append, pop) — LIFO
items.note: DFS is about going deep before going wide. Return values bubble up from leaves to root.

items.label: Trigger
items.code:
Keywords: all combinations, all permutations, generate all, subsets, partition
Structure: explore decision tree, prune invalid branches
items.note: If the problem asks to 'generate all' or 'find all', it is almost certainly backtracking.

items.label: Data Structures
items.code:
# result list to collect valid solutions
# path (current state being built)
# start index to avoid duplicates in combinations
result = []
path = []
items.note: Use start index for combinations (order doesn't matter). Don't use it for permutations.

items.label: Template
items.code:
def backtrack(start, path, result, nums):
    result.append(path[:])  # or check validity first
    for i in range(start, len(nums)):
        path.append(nums[i])
        backtrack(i + 1, path, result, nums)  # i+1: no reuse
        # backtrack(i, ...)   -> allow reuse
        # backtrack(0, ...)   -> permutation
        path.pop()  # undo choice

result = []
backtrack(0, [], result, nums)
return result
items.note: The three knobs: start=i+1 (subsets), start=i (reuse), start=0 (permutations).

items.label: Example 1 — LC 78 Subsets
items.note: Every node in the decision tree is a valid subset. Append at every call.

items.label: Example 2 — LC 39 Combination Sum
items.note: Pass i (not i+1) to allow reusing the same candidate. Prune when remaining < 0.

items.label: Key Insight
items.code:
# Subsets:       start=i+1, append at every node
# Combinations:  start=i+1, append when target met
# Combo w/reuse: start=i,   append when target met
# Permutations:  no start,  use 'used' boolean array
# Always: path.append -> recurse -> path.pop (backtrack)
items.note: Backtracking = DFS on a decision tree. Each level = one choice. Pop = undo that choice.

items.label: Trigger
items.code:
Keywords: minimum cost, maximum profit, number of ways, can you reach, longest
Structure: overlapping subproblems + optimal substructure
items.note: If brute force would recompute the same states, DP caches those results.

items.label: Data Structures
items.code:
# 1D DP: dp = [0] * (n + 1)
# 2D DP: dp = [[0] * (m + 1) for _ in range(n + 1)]
# Memoization: from functools import lru_cache
from functools import lru_cache
items.note: Bottom-up (tabulation) vs top-down (memoization). Both work; bottom-up avoids recursion limits.

items.label: Template
items.note: Define: 1) State, 2) Base case, 3) Recurrence, 4) Answer location.

items.label: Example 1 — LC 70 Climbing Stairs
items.note: dp[i] = ways to reach step i. Fibonacci variant. Can optimize to O(1) space with two variables.

items.label: Example 2 — LC 322 Coin Change
items.note: dp[i] = min coins for amount i. Try each coin, take min. Classic unbounded knapsack.

items.label: Key Insight
items.code:
# DP framework:
# 1. State: what info defines a subproblem? (index, remaining)
# 2. Choice: what decisions at each state?
# 3. Base case: smallest subproblem answer
# 4. Recurrence: how states relate (dp[i] = f(dp[j]))
# 5. Space optimization: often only need last row/few values
items.note: Start with brute-force recursion, add memoization, then convert to bottom-up if needed.

items.label: Trigger
items.code:
Keywords: subarray sum, range query, cumulative, sum equals k
Structure: need sum of any subarray in O(1) after preprocessing
items.note: Prefix sum turns range-sum queries from O(n) to O(1) with O(n) preprocessing.

items.label: Data Structures
items.code:
# Prefix sum array: prefix[i] = sum(nums[0:i])
# HashMap for 'subarray sum equals k' variant
from collections import defaultdict
prefix_count = defaultdict(int)
items.note: sum(nums[i:j]) = prefix[j] - prefix[i]. HashMap tracks how many times each prefix sum occurred.

items.label: Template
items.code:
# Build prefix sum
prefix = [0] * (len(nums) + 1)
for i in range(len(nums)):
    prefix[i + 1] = prefix[i] + nums[i]
# sum of nums[i:j] = prefix[j] - prefix[i]

# Subarray sum equals k (HashMap variant)
def subarraySum(nums, k):
    count = 0
    curr_sum = 0
    prefix_count = {0: 1}
    for num in nums:
        curr_sum += num
        count += prefix_count.get(curr_sum - k, 0)
        prefix_count[curr_sum] = prefix_count.get(curr_sum, 0) + 1
    return count
items.note: curr_sum - k in the map means a prefix with that sum exists, so the subarray between sums to k.

items.label: Example 1 — LC 560 Subarray Sum Equals K
items.note: O(n) time, O(n) space. Cannot use sliding window because nums can have negatives.

items.label: Example 2 — LC 238 Product of Array Except Self
items.code:
def productExceptSelf(nums):
    n = len(nums)
    ans = [1] * n
    left = 1
    for i in range(n):
        ans[i] = left
        left *= nums[i]
    right = 1
    for i in range(n - 1, -1, -1):
        ans[i] *= right
        right *= nums[i]
    return ans
items.note: Prefix product from left, then multiply suffix product from right. O(1) extra space.

items.label: Key Insight
items.code:
# Prefix sum + HashMap: if curr_sum - k exists in map,
# a subarray summing to k ends at current index.
# Same logic as Two Sum!
# Two Sum: target - num in map
# Subarray Sum: curr_sum - k in map
items.note: The prefix sum + HashMap combo is one of the most reusable tricks in interviews.

items.label: Trigger
items.code:
Keywords: merge, overlap, interval scheduling, meeting rooms, insert interval
Structure: list of [start, end] pairs
items.note: Almost all interval problems start with sorting by start time.

items.label: Data Structures
items.code:
# Sort intervals by start time
intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])
# Merged result list
merged = []
items.note: For 'minimum removals' problems, sort by end time instead (greedy).

items.label: Template
items.note: O(n log n) for sort. Two intervals overlap when start <= prev_end.

items.label: Example 1 — LC 56 Merge Intervals
items.note: Classic merge. Compare current start with last merged end.

items.label: Example 2 — LC 435 Non-Overlapping Intervals
items.note: Greedy: sort by end time, keep intervals that start after previous ends. Minimize removals.

items.label: Key Insight
items.code:
# Overlap check: a.start <= b.end and b.start <= a.end
# Merge: sort by start, extend end
# Min removals: sort by end (greedy — pick earliest ending)
# Insert: find position, merge with neighbors
items.note: Sort by start for merge problems. Sort by end for scheduling/removal problems.

items.label: Trigger
items.code:
Keywords: top k, kth largest, kth smallest, merge k sorted, median stream
Structure: need repeated access to min or max element
items.note: If you need the top-k or repeatedly extract the smallest/largest, use a heap.

items.label: Data Structures
items.code:
import heapq
heapq.heappush(heap, val)   # push — O(log n)
heapq.heappop(heap)         # pop smallest — O(log n)
heapq.heapify(arr)          # in-place — O(n)
# Max-heap trick: negate values
heapq.heappush(heap, -val)
items.note: Python only has min-heap. For max-heap, push -val and negate on pop.

items.label: Template (Top K)
items.code:
import heapq
def top_k_largest(nums, k):
    # Min-heap of size k -> top gives kth largest
    heap = []
    for num in nums:
        heapq.heappush(heap, num)
        if len(heap) > k:
            heapq.heappop(heap)  # remove smallest
    return heap[0]  # kth largest

# Or use nlargest/nsmallest
heapq.nlargest(k, nums)
heapq.nsmallest(k, nums)
items.note: For kth largest, keep a min-heap of size k. The top is the kth largest. O(n log k).

items.label: Example 1 — LC 703 Kth Largest Element in a Stream
items.note: Maintain a min-heap of size k. Top element is always the kth largest.

items.label: Example 2 — LC 347 Top K Frequent Elements
items.note: heapq.nlargest is clean. Bucket sort avoids the heap entirely for O(n).

items.label: Key Insight
items.code:
# Kth largest  -> min-heap of size k (top = answer)
# Kth smallest -> max-heap of size k (top = answer)
# Merge k lists -> heap of (value, list_idx, elem_idx)
# Running median -> two heaps (max-heap left, min-heap right)
items.note: Heap gives O(log k) per operation. For top-k problems, total is O(n log k).

items.label: Trigger
items.code:
Keywords: duplicate, frequency, group by, anagram, contains, two sum, seen before
Structure: need O(1) lookup, counting, or grouping
items.note: HashMap is the most commonly used data structure in interviews. Master it.

items.label: Data Structures
items.code:
from collections import defaultdict, Counter
freq = Counter(nums)          # frequency count
groups = defaultdict(list)     # grouping
seen = set()                   # dedup / O(1) lookup
items.note: Counter is a dict subclass. defaultdict avoids KeyError. Set for membership tests.

items.label: Template
items.note: The 'complement lookup' pattern (Two Sum) appears everywhere. Store what you've seen, check for complement.

items.label: Example 1 — LC 1 Two Sum
items.note: O(n) time, O(n) space. One pass — check then store.

items.label: Example 2 — LC 36 Valid Sudoku
items.code:
def isValidSudoku(board):
    rows = defaultdict(set)
    cols = defaultdict(set)
    boxes = defaultdict(set)
    for r in range(9):
        for c in range(9):
            val = board[r][c]
            if val == '.':
                continue
            box_id = (r // 3) * 3 + (c // 3)
            if (val in rows[r] or val in cols[c]
                    or val in boxes[box_id]):
                return False
            rows[r].add(val)
            cols[c].add(val)
            boxes[box_id].add(val)
    return True
items.note: box_id = (row // 3) * 3 + (col // 3) maps each cell to its 3x3 box (0-8). Three sets track uniqueness.

items.label: Key Insight
items.code:
# HashMap patterns:
# 1. Complement lookup: target - val in map (Two Sum)
# 2. Frequency count: Counter(nums) then process
# 3. Group by key: defaultdict(list), group by sorted/hash
# 4. Seen tracking: set for visited/duplicate detection
# 5. Index mapping: val -> index for O(1) position lookup
items.note: When stuck, ask: 'Can I store something to avoid a nested loop?' Usually yes, with a HashMap.

items.label: Trigger
items.code:
Keywords: locally optimal, scheduling, maximum/minimum with greedy choice, jump game
Structure: making the best local choice leads to global optimum
items.note: Greedy works when the problem has the 'greedy choice property' — local optimum leads to global optimum.

items.label: Data Structures
items.code:
# Often just variables tracking state
# Sometimes sorted input
# No special data structure — greedy is a strategy, not a structure
items.note: Greedy problems are about proving the strategy works, not about complex data structures.

items.label: Template
items.code:
# General greedy approach:
# 1. Sort input (if needed)
# 2. Iterate and make locally optimal choice
# 3. Update state
# 4. Never reconsider past choices

def greedy(items):
    items.sort(key=some_criteria)
    state = initial_value
    for item in items:
        if can_take(item, state):
            state = update(state, item)
    return state
items.note: The hard part is proving greedy works. If you can't prove it, consider DP instead.

items.label: Example 1 — LC 55 Jump Game
items.note: Track the farthest index reachable. If current index > max_reach, stuck. O(n) time.

items.label: Example 2 — LC 134 Gas Station
items.note: If total gas >= total cost, solution exists. Reset start whenever tank goes negative.

items.label: Key Insight
items.code:
# Greedy vs DP:
# Greedy: make one choice per step, never look back
# DP: consider all choices, pick best via recurrence
# Greedy is faster but only works with greedy choice property
# Common greedy patterns:
#   - Activity selection (sort by end time)
#   - Jump game (track farthest reach)
#   - Gas station (reset on failure)
items.note: If the greedy approach gives wrong answers on edge cases, switch to DP.

items.label: Trigger
items.code:
Keywords: connected components, group, union, redundant connection, accounts merge
Structure: disjoint sets that get merged over time
items.note: Union-Find is ideal when you process edges one at a time and need to check/merge components.

items.label: Data Structures
items.code:
# Parent array + rank array
# Path compression + union by rank -> near O(1) per operation
parent = list(range(n))
rank = [0] * n
items.note: Amortized O(alpha(n)) per operation — effectively constant.

items.label: Template
items.note: find() with path compression. union() by rank. Track component count.

items.label: Example 1 — LC 323 Number of Connected Components
items.note: Start with n components, each union merges two. Final count is the answer.

items.label: Example 2 — LC 684 Redundant Connection
items.note: The first edge connecting two already-connected nodes is the redundant one.

items.label: Key Insight
items.code:
# Union-Find vs DFS/BFS for connected components:
# - DFS/BFS: build full graph, traverse -> O(V + E)
# - Union-Find: process edges incrementally -> O(E * alpha(V))
# Union-Find wins when:
#   - Edges arrive one at a time (streaming)
#   - Need to detect cycles in undirected graph
#   - Need component count after each edge
items.note: Union-Find for dynamic connectivity. DFS/BFS for static graphs.

items.label: Trigger
items.code:
Keywords: prefix search, autocomplete, word dictionary, starts with, word search II
Structure: tree where each edge is a character, shared prefixes
items.note: Trie is the go-to for prefix-based problems. HashMap of children at each node.

items.label: Data Structures
items.note: Each node has up to 26 children (for lowercase English). is_end marks complete words.

items.label: Template
items.note: insert: O(m), search: O(m), startsWith: O(m) where m = word length.

items.label: Example 1 — LC 208 Implement Trie
items.note: Exact implementation tested on LeetCode. Clean and minimal.

items.label: Example 2 — LC 212 Word Search II
items.note: Trie + DFS backtracking. Trie prunes branches that can't form any word.

items.label: Key Insight
items.code:
# Trie vs HashMap for prefix problems:
# HashMap: exact word O(1) but prefix search O(n*m)
# Trie: prefix search O(m), shared prefixes save space
# When to use Trie:
#   - Multiple prefix queries
#   - Autocomplete / typeahead
#   - Word search in grid (prunes DFS branches)
#   - Longest common prefix
items.note: Trie is a prefix tree. If the problem involves prefixes of multiple words, Trie is the answer.
